data.title: FAA probing close call at JFK airport
data.description:
The Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) is looking into a close call between two planes at John F. Kennedy International Airport, the agency said in a statement.

According to the FAA statement, Republic Airways flight 4464 halted its landing at JFK on Monday afternoon “after missing the intended approach path and flying too close to Jazz Aviation Flight 554,” a flight that was set to touch down “on a parallel runway.”

“The FAA is investigating the event that occurred around 2:35 p.m. local time on Monday, April 20,” the agency said.

Just last month, the FAA said it was investigating a helicopter that crossed in front of a commercial plane in California. United Airlines flight 589 was headed to John Wayne Airport in Santa Ana, Calif., late last month as a Sikorsky Black Hawk helicopter crossed its path.

In January of last year, a notable and deadly collision between an American Airlines flight and a Black Hawk helicopter near Reagan Washington National Airport killed 67 people. The D.C.-area collision and other recent aviation incidents prompted wider conversation about the safety of flying.

The FAA has since tightened helicopter safety measures, with FAA Administrator Bryan Bedford and Transportation Secretary Sean Duffy saying last month that the Trump administration was halting the use of visual separation between airplanes and helicopters and requiring that air traffic controllers use radar to “actively manage these aircraft to keep them separated at specific lateral or vertical distances.”

data.title: Rogan: Trump 'called me a liberal' at White House
data.description:
Podcaster Joe Rogan on Tuesday said President Trump “called me a liberal” while at a White House event last week to hasten research on psychedelic drugs, including ibogaine.

“The Joe Rogan Experience” guest Luis J. Gomez told Rogan that “people are pissed” about remarks Trump made during the event, where Trump signed an executive order on psychedelic drugs. Gomez said people online were asking about Rogan previously calling himself “politically homeless.”

“I am,” Rogan replied. “[Trump] joked about it. He called me a liberal during the whole thing.

The podcast host, in an impression of Trump, added that the president said, “‘Joe, he’s very liberal.'”

Rogan spoke about the benefits of ibogaine at the event. He said he texted Trump before the event with information about the psychedelic drug. According to the podcast host, the president responded: “Sounds great. Do you want FDA [Food and Drug Administration] approval? Let’s do it.”

After he finished speaking, Trump said, “We all respect Joe and –– he’s a little bit more liberal than I am. But that’s OK. I have a lot of friends that are liberal.”

Rogan, who endorsed Trump the day before the 2024 presidential election, has disagreed with Trump over the U.S.-Israeli conflict in Iran. He called the situation last week “f—ing terrifying” and asked, “What the f— are we doing?”

“Most people that voted for Trump or wanted Trump to be in office, one of the things that was attractive was this, no more wars,” Rogan said. “Now we’re in one of the craziest ones.”

Earlier this month, Rogan told podcaster Theo Von that when “we started bombing Iran, I was like, ‘This can’t be true.'”

Rogan said in the days after the conflict began that the president’s supporters “feel betrayed.”

“Well, it just seems so insane, based on what he ran on. I mean, this is why a lot of people feel betrayed, right? he said. “He ran on, ‘No more wars,’ ‘End these stupid, senseless wars,’ and then we have one that we can’t even really clearly define why we did it.”

The president extended the ceasefire between the U.S. and Iran to give Iran time to submit a peace proposal in an upcoming second round of talks with negotiators in Islamabad, Pakistan. Trump said these talks could happen as early as Friday.

It is unclear if Iran will commit to meeting for the negotiations. Iran shut down the Strait of Hormuz for a third time since the conflict began after Trump deployed a blockade to prevent vessels to pass.

Trump said Tuesday that the blockade will remain and that the U.S. military will “remain ready and able… until such time as [Iran’s] proposal is submitted and discussions are concluded, one way or the other.”

data.title: Navy Secretary stepping down 13 months into job
data.description:
Navy Secretary John Phelan is departing the administration after serving in the role for just over a year, the Pentagon announced on Wednesday. 

“On behalf of the Secretary of War and Deputy Secretary of War, we are grateful to Secretary Phelan for his service to the Department and the United States Navy,” Sean Parnell, the Pentagon’s chief spokesperson, said in a statement. 

“We wish him well in his future endeavors,” Parnell said. 

Undersecretary Hung Cao, a Navy veteran and former Senate candidate in Virginia, will serve as the acting Secretary of the Navy.

Phelan, who was confirmed by the Senate in March last year, is the latest in a string of high-profile departures from the Pentagon under Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth.

Developing story

data.title: Kalshi suspends 3 political candidates for betting on own races
data.description:
Kalshi has suspended three political candidates for betting on their own races, the prediction market platform announced Wednesday.

Minnesota congressional candidate and state Sen. Matt Klein (D) and former Texas congressional candidate Ezekiel Enriquez (R) both settled and paid small fines over the trades, while Virginia Senate candidate Mark Moran (I) declined to settle and is facing a $6,000 fine. All three were suspended from the prediction market for five years.

“All three cases concern political insider trading and were flagged because of our newly released safeguards to block political candidates from trading on their own elections,” Robert DeNault, head of enforcement and legal counsel at Kalshi, wrote on the social platform X.

“Kalshi does not tolerate anyone cheating or skirting the rules,” he continued. “Regulated exchanges must constantly evolve and adapt their systems to address insider threats. These three cases are an example of how developing proactive engineering solutions can help identify illicit trading activity, and how individuals who cooperate and take responsibility can mitigate penalties.”

Kalshi found that Klein, who is running in the Democratic primary for Minnesota’s 2nd congressional district, purchased less than $100 worth of contracts on his own candidacy. He agreed to settle and was fined about $500.

Enriquez, who lost in the Republican primary for Texas’s 21st congressional district in March, also spent less than $100 trading on his own candidacy. He settled with the platform, which is imposing a fine of nearly $800.

Moran placed wagers on his own candidacy in two markets, one on individuals who would run for office in 2026 and another on the Virginia Democratic Senate nominee. After placing trades on the second market, he also promoted it on social media, according to Kalshi.

While he acknowledged that the trades “were improper and in violation of the Kalshi exchange rules,” he “repeatedly refused to resolve this matter via settlement and stopped responding to further correspondence from the Compliance Department,” the platform noted.

In an X post Wednesday, Moran said he bet on himself on Kalshi because he “wanted to get caught.”

“I traded $100 on myself, knowing this would happen (also knowing that I wouldn’t be vying for the democratic nomination) and the attention it would create to highlight how this company is destroying young men and as Senator I will go after Kalshi and impose significant penalties on them – 25% – a vice tax – to pay down our national debt,” he added.

The recent enforcement actions come as Kalshi has sought to crack down on insider trading on its platform in the face of increasing congressional scrutiny on prediction markets.

It announced in March that it was launching “new technological guardrails” to block politicians, athletes and other relevant people trading in certain markets.

Fellow prediction market platform Polymarket also unveiled “updated market integrity rules” in March to clarify prohibited insider trading conduct, including trading on stolen confidential information and illegal tips or by those who can influence the outcome.

data.title: Senate Democrats press USPS to reject Trump mail-in voting order
data.description:
Dozens of Senate Democrats are calling on the U.S. Postal Service not to comply with President Trump’s executive order that restricts mail-in voting.

Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer (D-N.Y.) and Sens. Gary Peters (D-Mich.), Alex Padilla (D-Calif.) and Dick Durbin (D-Ill.) demanded in a Monday letter to the Postal Service that it “follow the law and not implement” the order, which it cast as a “blatant violation of the Constitution and statutes governing postal operations.”

“Like the President, the Postal Service has no authority to regulate the manner of voting in federal elections, nor who is eligible to vote by mail in such elections,” senators wrote.

The letter, signed by 37 Democratic senators in total, was first reported by Reuters.

The order, signed earlier this month, directs Homeland Security Secretary Markwayne Mullin to work with the Social Security Administration to compile a list of verified U.S. citizens who are eligible to vote.

It bars the U.S. Postal Service from sending ballots to those not on the state’s approved mail-in ballot list and requires ballots to be secured in envelopes with barcodes for tracking.

More than 20 Democratic-led states quickly sued in an attempt to block the order from taking effect. Schumer and House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries (D-N.Y.) also filed a separate lawsuit alongside the Democratic National Committee and other party organizations.

Senators argued in their letter that Trump “is attempting to unconstitutionally consolidate power to personally regulate American elections,” warning that his order could disenfranchise voters.

“These directives clearly infringe on the states’ and Congress’ constitutional role to regulate the manner in which federal elections are held and would deny eligible voters their ability to cast a ballot,” they wrote.

Trump has sought in his second term to use his authority to exert more control over federal elections, often claiming that practices such as mail-in voting contributed to widespread fraud in the 2020 presidential election despite a lack of supporting evidence.

Ahead of the November midterms, he has called for nationalizing elections and pressured Republicans to pass the SAVE America Act, which would require Americans to show photo identification when voting in federal elections and provide proof of U.S. citizenship to register.

data.title: Massie says GOP 'wasted' $10M in his primary: 'Imagine if they had used that in Virginia'
data.description:
Rep. Thomas Massie (R-Ky.) said the Republican Party “wasted” the money it spent funding his opponent and attack ads against him in the upcoming Kentucky Republican primary race. 

“They’ve wasted $10 million in my race already. Imagine if they had used that in Virginia,” Massie wrote on the social platform X, referencing Democrats’ Tuesday redistricting win in the state. 

President Trump has backed Ed Gallrein to challenge Massie in Kentucky’s Republican primary election on May 19. Trump went to the state last month to speak in support of Gallrein.

Massie frequently butts heads with the Trump administration, criticizing their handling of the files related to convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein and opposing the GOP’s massive funding bill passed last year. 

Gallrein has raised over $2 million in his contest against the Republican incumbent, mostly from individual donations. Nearly $100,000 of his campaign funding has come through the Republican Jewish Coalition PAC, according to Federal Election Committee (FEC) filings.

Additionally, Trump-linked super PAC MAGA KY has raised over $1 million for political ads targeting Massie. 

Massie has raised over double his Republican challenger, drawing in nearly $5 million for his campaign, according to filings with the FEC. 

Virginia voters narrowly approved new congressional maps Tuesday that could swing four additional House seats in Democrats’ favor in the upcoming midterm elections. 

Democrats spent around $65 million, including nearly $39 million from House Majority Forward, a nonprofit group aligned with congressional Democratic leadership. The Republican funding effort against the maps raised nearly $22 million. Trump’s super PAC, MAGA Inc., spent no money on the redistricting fight. 

The president’s political director, James Blair, deflected criticism about the GOP’s comparative lack of funding in this election during a Wednesday interview with CNN, casting doubt on the effectiveness of these new maps for Democrats. 

“These are not slam dunk districts for them,” Blair said. “They unloaded a lot of cash, and they’re going to have to fight for them in November if they get past the courts.”

MAGA Inc. has raised over $330 million ahead of the midterm elections, according the most recent FEC filing data from the end of March. Blair refused to say exactly how the Trump administration planned to spend this money when asked by CNN.

“Well, I’m certainly not going to broadcast our battle plans to the other side through the media,” Blair said on Wednesday. “But what I would say is I don’t think Republicans will be at a spending disadvantage in [these] midterms … and in fact, I firmly believe they will have a significant spending advantage.”

data.title: House Democrat: Iran in 'better position' today than before war
data.description:
Rep. Seth Moulton (D-Mass.) on Tuesday said Iran is in a “better position” than it was before the U.S.-Israeli conflict against Iran started on Feb. 28.

Moulton, an outspoken critic of the war, told CNN’s Anderson Cooper that Iran has “the willingness to endure the pain and stick this out.”

“They are in a better position today than they were before this war started,” he continued. “I mean, remember what was happening right before Trump started his war of choice. We were negotiating with the Iranians to curtail their nuclear weapons program. We were asking them to cut back on their nuclear weapons development.”

President Trump pulled the U.S. out of the 2015 Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action in 2018, during his first term. The deal, penned during the administration of former President Obama, required the Islamic Republic to dismantle its nuclear program in exchange for sanctions relief. 

The U.S. and Iran held negotiations over the nuclear program leading up to the U.S. and Israeli strikes that started on Feb. 28. Those negotiations fell apart amid rising tensions and Trump’s condemnation of the Iranian leadership during its crackdown on anti-government protests.

Trump and his administration’s case in the days following the start of the conflict was that Iran posed an “imminent nuclear threat.” He and other administration officials have called for Iran to end its nuclear program, which Iranian officials have said has been devoted to “peaceful purposes.”

Moulton earlier this month accused Trump of “resorting to war crimes” in his threats to decimate Iran’s energy infrastructure. He contrasted the strategy in Iran with former President George W. Bush’s strategy in Iraq over 20 years ago. The war saw growing unpopularity in the years after it started, but Moulton praised the Bush administration, “for all the problems with that war, at least there was a plan.”

The Massachusetts Democrat later credited Iran with “winning the war” after its anti-aircraft defenses shot down two U.S. fighter jets.

Trump indefinitely extended a two-week long ceasefire with Iran to give the country time to submit a peace plan. He indicated on Wednesday that the negotiations could come as early as Friday, with Vice President Vance, special envoy Steve Witkoff and Trump’s son-in-law Jared Kushner leading the talks.

Iran has not confirmed that it will take part in the second rounds of talks in Islamabad, Pakistan. Pakistani Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif’s office stated that he met with Iranian Ambassador Reza Amiri Moghadam on Wednesday to “discuss the ongoing regional situation and peace efforts.”

data.title: Debate Night Live: Race for California governor
data.description:
The Hill will present two and a half hours of live coverage on Nexstar Media Inc.’s California gubernatorial primary debate on Wednesday evening.

The debate will be moderated by Nikki Laurenzo, news anchor at KTXL FOX40 in Sacramento and host of Inside California Politics, and Frank Buckley, veteran morning news anchor at KTLA in Los Angeles.

The six candidates who met the polling threshold, earning at least 5 percent in Nexstar’s March statewide survey, include Riverside County Sheriff Chad Bianco (R), former Fox News host Steve Hilton (R), former Rep. Katie Porter (D-Calif.), former Health and Human Services Secretary Xavier Becerra (D), San Jose Mayor Matt Mahan (D) and billionaire Tom Steyer (D). All candidates have accepted Nexstar’s invitation to participate.

The Hill’s coverage will be anchored by “Sunrise on The Hill” host Cory Smith, joined by The Hill’s political editor and NewsNation host Chris Stirewalt, with further contributions from The Hill campaign team, Decision Desk HQ, Nexstar’s DC Bureau and NewsNation reporters, plus political analysts.

The special will feature live analysis leading into and after the debate. Viewers can expect sharp insight into what the outcomes mean for the political landscape ahead.

Coverage begins at 9:30 p.m. EDT and will run until midnight.

Watch above.

data.title: Trump administration giving unclear graduate student loan guidance: Financial aid group
data.description:
The Department of Education (DoE) is issuing unclear guidance on whether graduate school loans are included in the new borrowing limit that will take effect this summer, according to the National Association of Student Financial Aid Administrators (NASFAA). 

In a Tuesday release, the financial aid group said that the DoE on Monday reversed course and said that Graduate PLUS loans will be included in the new $257,500 lifetime borrowing limit, which was established under the One Big Beautiful Bill (OBBB) Act and goes into effects on July 1. 

Starting that day, the Graduate PLUS loan program will be phased out, with certain exceptions. 

Graduate or professional students that were enrolled in a program as of June 30, borrowed a direct PLUS loan for that program prior to July 1, are enrolled in that same program and have not had any break in their enrollment — outside of approved leave of absences — are still eligible to receive such loans, according to studentaid.gov. 

In the “Reimagining and Improving Student Education” regulations the DoE posted to the Federal Register in January, the department said that the lifetime limit excludes Federal PLUS or Federal Direct PLUS loans. 

But this week, the DoE “revised” its position and said that Graduate PLUS loans count against the limit, citing a “new interpretation of the statute,” according to NASFAA.

Melanie Storey, the president and CEO of the financial aid group, said Tuesday that the reversal “only adds to an already confusing and rapidly shifting policy landscape” for student loan borrowers. 

“With many schools preparing to issue financial aid offers in the coming weeks — and some doing so this month — this approach is both irresponsible and unfair to students and financial aid professionals who are working in good faith to make informed decisions amid inconsistent and incomplete information,” Storey added. 

“When significant policy changes are rolled out without clear, formal, and widely distributed guidance, there are severe consequences that directly affect students who need definitive answers now in order to make plans to pay for college.”

Storey urged the DoE to “immediately issue clear, formal guidance” on the matter and “prioritize timely, transparent communication moving forward.” 

The Hill has reached out to the DoE for comment.

From October 2023 to January 2024, more than 6 in 10 student loan borrowers reported having difficulty making their payments at any point, with 37 percent saying they missed at least one payment, according to a survey conducted by the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau.

data.title: Purse thief who targeted Noem sentenced
data.description:
A man who pleaded guilty to stealing former Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem’s purse at a downtown Washington, D.C., restaurant in a string of thefts last spring has been sentenced to three years in prison.

Mario Bustamante Leiva, 50, received a 36-month sentence in federal court on Wednesday, according to the U.S. Attorney for the District of Columbia.

He pleaded guilty in November to three counts of wire fraud and one count of first-degree theft. Pirro’s office said he is living in the country illegally and will be subject to deportation once his sentence is completed.

“Bustamante Leiva came to Washington illegally to prey on citizens of the District. He methodically targeted women at restaurants, stealing their purses, and monetizing the stolen cards within minutes,” Pirro said in a statement. “His pattern of theft ends here. He will serve his prison term and be deported.” 

Federal court filings stated that across three separate days last April, Bustamente Leiva would “target diners at restaurants in the District of Columbia and elsewhere, stealthily steal their purses, and make fraudulent purchases using the diner’s stolen credit cards.”

One of those incidents involved Noem, who was dining with her family at Capital Burger on 7th Street NW to celebrate Easter.  

Prosecutors said Bustamante Leiva entered the restaurant and “deliberately seated himself within arm’s reach of his next victim,” whom he did not know at the time was the then-secretary. Surveillance video then captured him repeatedly looking down toward Noem’s purse before “bending down and snatching it.”

The bag contained $3,000 in cash, her Department of Homeland Security access badge, her passport, her driver’s license and several credit cards, among other items.

“After the theft, Bustamante Leiva traveled to a restaurant in the 2900 block of M Street NW, where surveillance cameras recorded him in possession of Noem’s purse, wallet, and at least one of her credit cards, which he used to make an unauthorized purchase,” Pirro’s office stated.

Bustamante Leiva remained at the restaurant until roughly midnight, when he exited and fell asleep at an outdoor table, according to charging documents.

He was arrested at a motel a few days later after authorities reportedly tracked his location through gift card purchases made on another victim’s stolen credit card. Noem’s purse and wallet, several thousand dollars in cash and other items were found during a search of his room.

Prosecutors said that Bustamente Leiva entered the U.S. in August 2021 on a visa authorizing him to stay in the country until November.

“He did not leave and continued to reside in the country illegally,” Pirro’s office stated, adding that he also has active criminal warrants in Utah and New York.

His co-defendant, Cristian Montecino-Sanzana, was sentenced in March to 13 months in prison and three years of supervised release for his role in one of the thefts, according to the office.
